stem: A 48-year-old man presents to his primary care physician because his wife reports that he stops breathing during sleep. He has been increasingly tired during the day and has fallen asleep at work twice in the past month. His BMI is 34 kg/m2, blood pressure is 152/94 mm Hg, and neck circumference is 44 cm. Physical examination reveals a crowded oropharynx. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?

explanation.summary: This patient has classic features of obstructive sleep apnea (witnessed apneas, excessive daytime sleepiness, obesity, hypertension, large neck circumference). Polysomnography is the gold standard diagnostic test and must be performed before initiating treatment.
explanation.whyCorrect: Polysomnography (PSG) is the gold standard for diagnosing sleep apnea. It measures brain activity, oxygen saturation, and respiratory effort during sleep and provides the apnea-hypopnea index (AHI), which is essential for confirming the diagnosis and determining severity before treatment is initiated.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CPAP is the first-line treatment for OSA, but the diagnosis must be confirmed with polysomnography before initiating therapy.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Modafinil may be used as adjunctive therapy for residual daytime sleepiness in OSA patients already on CPAP. It does not address the underlying airway obstruction.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Surgery is reserved for patients with specific anatomical abnormalities who have failed or cannot tolerate CPAP. It is not appropriate before establishing a diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Weight loss is an important lifestyle modification but alone is insufficient. The patient needs diagnostic confirmation and definitive treatment given his significant symptoms.

stem: A 55-year-old obese man is diagnosed with obstructive sleep apnea after polysomnography reveals an apnea-hypopnea index of 38 events per hour. He is started on continuous positive airway pressure (CPAP) therapy. Which of the following best describes the mechanism by which CPAP improves his condition?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Maintains upper airway patency with pressure

explanation.summary: CPAP works by delivering air at a pressure sufficient to overcome pharyngeal collapse, thereby maintaining patency of the upper airway during sleep and preventing obstructive events.
explanation.whyCorrect: CPAP delivers continuous positive pressure through a mask that pneumatically splints the upper airway open. This prevents the pharyngeal collapse that occurs during sleep in patients with OSA, maintaining airway patency and allowing unobstructed ventilation throughout the night.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CPAP delivers room air at positive pressure, not supplemental oxygen. Its primary mechanism is maintaining airway patency, not increasing the fraction of inspired oxygen.
whyWrongByChoice.B: CPAP does not stimulate brainstem respiratory centers. It mechanically prevents airway collapse. Central respiratory drive stimulation would be relevant for central sleep apnea, not OSA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: CPAP does not directly stimulate pharyngeal muscles. It works by providing a pneumatic splint that keeps the airway open regardless of muscle tone.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While treating OSA may eventually improve pulmonary pressures, the primary therapeutic mechanism is mechanical maintenance of airway patency.

stem: A 60-year-old man with a history of congestive heart failure (ejection fraction 25%) is evaluated for excessive daytime sleepiness. His wife reports progressively deeper breathing followed by periods of no breathing during sleep. A sleep study shows central apneas without evidence of upper airway obstruction. Which of the following breathing patterns is most likely observed?

explanation.summary: Cheyne-Stokes respiration is a pattern of cyclically waxing and waning tidal volumes with interposed central apneas, classically associated with severe heart failure and central sleep apnea.
explanation.whyCorrect: Cheyne-Stokes respiration is characterized by a crescendo-decrescendo pattern of breathing alternating with periods of apnea. It is strongly associated with heart failure because prolonged circulation time delays the detection of blood gas changes by central chemoreceptors, causing an oscillating overshoot-undershoot cycle in ventilation.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Biot breathing (ataxic breathing) consists of irregular and unpredictable periods of apnea alternating with breathing. It is typically associated with brainstem damage, not heart failure.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Kussmaul breathing is deep, rapid, and labored breathing in response to metabolic acidosis (e.g., diabetic ketoacidosis). It is not associated with central sleep apnea.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Obstructive hypopneas involve partial airflow reduction due to upper airway narrowing with continued respiratory effort. This patient has central apneas without obstruction.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Paradoxical breathing occurs when the chest wall and abdomen move in opposite directions, seen in diaphragmatic paralysis or severe upper airway obstruction, not CSA with heart failure.

stem: A 4-year-old boy is brought to the pediatrician because of loud snoring, mouth breathing during sleep, and daytime behavioral problems at preschool. On examination, he has tonsillar hypertrophy graded 3+ bilaterally. Polysomnography confirms obstructive sleep apnea with an AHI of 8. Which of the following is the most appropriate first-line treatment?

explanation.summary: Adenotonsillectomy is the first-line treatment for OSA in children with adenotonsillar hypertrophy. It improves daytime behavior, decreases sleepiness, and increases quality of life.
explanation.whyCorrect: In children, the most common cause of OSA is adenotonsillar hypertrophy. Adenotonsillectomy is recommended first-line therapy because it directly addresses the anatomical cause and has been shown to improve symptoms and quality of life.

whyWrongByChoice.B: CPAP is the first-line therapy for adults with OSA, not children with adenotonsillar hypertrophy. Surgery is preferred when a correctable cause is identified.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Intranasal corticosteroids may have a role in mild pediatric OSA but are not first-line when significant adenotonsillar hypertrophy is present.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mandibular advancement devices are used in adults with mild to moderate OSA. They are not appropriate for a 4-year-old child.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Weight loss counseling is relevant for obese patients. This child's OSA is caused by adenotonsillar hypertrophy requiring surgical correction.

stem: A 50-year-old man with newly diagnosed obstructive sleep apnea asks why his physician is concerned about his blood pressure. His polysomnography showed an AHI of 25 with oxygen desaturation to 78% during events. Which of the following best explains how OSA contributes to systemic hypertension?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Decreased cardiac output during apneic episodes

choices.label: E
choices.text: Upper airway inflammation extending to vasculature

explanation.summary: Recurrent episodes of intermittent hypoxia during obstructive events trigger sympathetic nervous system activation, leading to systemic vasoconstriction and sustained hypertension.
explanation.whyCorrect: Repetitive cycles of hypoxia and reoxygenation activate the sympathetic nervous system through peripheral chemoreceptor stimulation. This results in elevated catecholamine levels, vasoconstriction, and an inflammatory response contributing to sustained systemic hypertension even during waking hours.

whyWrongByChoice.A: OSA causes intermittent hypoxia and hypercapnia, not chronic respiratory alkalosis. Hyperventilation is not a feature of obstructive sleep apnea.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While cardiac output may transiently decrease during severe apneas, the primary mechanism of hypertension is sympathetically mediated vasoconstriction from intermittent hypoxia.
whyWrongByChoice.D: OSA is associated with intermittent hypercapnia, but this does not suppress the renin-angiotensin system. Sympathetic activation tends to stimulate this system.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While OSA is associated with systemic inflammation, the primary mechanism of hypertension is sympathetic activation from intermittent hypoxia.

stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with excessive daytime sleepiness and morning headaches. She denies snoring but her husband reports frequent pauses in breathing during sleep. She has a BMI of 23 kg/m2 and a normal oropharyngeal examination. Her history is significant for a brainstem stroke 6 months ago. A sleep study shows frequent apneas without respiratory effort. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Apneas without respiratory effort on polysomnography indicate central sleep apnea. Her brainstem stroke is a recognized cause of CSA due to impaired central respiratory drive.
explanation.whyCorrect: Central sleep apnea is characterized by cessation of airflow without respiratory effort, distinguishing it from OSA where effort against a closed airway is present. Brainstem strokes can damage respiratory centers, impairing the CNS drive to breathe. Her normal BMI and oropharynx further argue against an obstructive etiology.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Narcolepsy causes excessive daytime sleepiness but is characterized by cataplexy, hypnagogic hallucinations, and sleep paralysis, not apneic episodes without respiratory effort.
whyWrongByChoice.C: OSA is characterized by apneas with continued respiratory effort against a collapsed upper airway. This patient has apneas without effort.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Obesity hypoventilation syndrome requires obesity (BMI >=30) with chronic daytime hypercapnia. This patient has a normal BMI of 23.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Upper airway resistance syndrome involves increased respiratory effort against a partially obstructed airway, not apneas without respiratory effort.

stem: A 62-year-old man with a BMI of 42 kg/m2 presents with worsening dyspnea, daytime somnolence, and lower extremity edema. Arterial blood gas on room air shows pH 7.34, PaCO2 52 mm Hg, PaO2 58 mm Hg, and HCO3 30 mEq/L. Pulmonary function tests show a mild restrictive pattern. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?

explanation.summary: Obesity hypoventilation syndrome (Pickwickian syndrome) is defined by obesity (BMI >=30), chronic daytime hypoventilation (PaCO2 >45 mm Hg), and hypoxemia not attributed to another condition. The elevated HCO3 confirms chronicity.
explanation.whyCorrect: This patient meets all criteria for OHS: morbid obesity (BMI 42), chronic daytime hypercapnia (PaCO2 52), and hypoxemia (PaO2 58). The elevated bicarbonate indicates metabolic compensation for chronic respiratory acidosis. The restrictive PFT pattern is consistent with decreased chest wall compliance from obesity.

whyWrongByChoice.A: COPD causes an obstructive pattern on PFTs, not the restrictive pattern seen here. COPD is typically associated with smoking, not morbid obesity.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While heart failure can cause dyspnea and edema, it does not typically present with chronic hypercapnia and a restrictive PFT pattern.
whyWrongByChoice.C: IPF causes a restrictive pattern but presents with fine bibasilar crackles and does not cause chronic hypercapnia in early stages.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Pulmonary embolism causes acute hypoxemia and hypocapnia from hyperventilation, not chronic hypercapnia with metabolic compensation.

stem: A 58-year-old man is being evaluated for resistant hypertension despite adherence to three antihypertensive medications. His wife reports loud snoring and breathing pauses during sleep. He is obese with a BMI of 33 kg/m2. Which of the following is the most likely underlying cause of his resistant hypertension?

explanation.summary: Obstructive sleep apnea is a common and often underrecognized cause of secondary (resistant) hypertension. The combination of snoring, witnessed apneas, and obesity strongly suggests OSA.
explanation.whyCorrect: OSA is one of the most common causes of resistant hypertension. Intermittent hypoxia triggers sustained sympathetic activation and inflammatory responses contributing to hypertension refractory to standard pharmacotherapy. This patient has classic OSA risk factors and symptoms.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Cushing syndrome can cause hypertension but typically presents with moon facies, striae, muscle weakness, and glucose intolerance.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Pheochromocytoma causes episodic hypertension with paroxysmal headaches, diaphoresis, and palpitations. The clinical picture here is more consistent with OSA.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Primary hyperaldosteronism causes resistant hypertension but the sleep-related symptoms make OSA the most likely diagnosis.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Renal artery stenosis can cause resistant hypertension but there is no abdominal bruit or renal insufficiency described. The sleep symptoms point to OSA.

stem: A 52-year-old man undergoes polysomnography for suspected sleep apnea. The study reveals 22 episodes per hour of complete airflow cessation lasting at least 10 seconds, all associated with continued chest and abdominal wall movement. Which of the following best characterizes these findings?

explanation.summary: Complete cessation of airflow with continued respiratory effort (chest and abdominal movement) defines obstructive apneas. An AHI of 22 falls in the moderate range (15-30 events/hour).
explanation.whyCorrect: Continued respiratory effort despite absent airflow is the hallmark of obstructive apneas. An AHI of 22 classifies as moderate severity (mild: 5-15, moderate: 15-30, severe: >30).

whyWrongByChoice.A: Central apneas are characterized by absence of both airflow and respiratory effort. This patient has continued chest and abdominal movement.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Mixed apneas have an initial central component followed by an obstructive component. All events here show continuous effort. Also, 22/hour is moderate, not mild.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Severe OSA is AHI greater than 30. This patient has an AHI of 22, which is moderate.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Hypopneas are partial reductions in airflow, not complete cessation. These events involve complete cessation.

stem: A 65-year-old man with NYHA class III heart failure and central sleep apnea has persistent symptoms despite optimization of heart failure therapy. His sleep study shows an AHI of 35 with predominantly central apneas. Which of the following is the most appropriate initial treatment for his sleep-disordered breathing?

explanation.summary: CPAP is the initial positive airway pressure therapy recommended for central sleep apnea associated with heart failure, as it can reduce central apneas and improve cardiac function.
explanation.whyCorrect: For CSA due to heart failure, CPAP is recommended as initial positive pressure therapy. CPAP reduces preload and afterload, improving cardiac function while stabilizing ventilation.

whyWrongByChoice.B: Acetazolamide has been studied for altitude-related CSA but is not standard first-line therapy for heart failure-related CSA.
whyWrongByChoice.C: BiPAP may be considered for CSA patients who fail CPAP, but CPAP is the initial therapy to try.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Mandibular advancement devices are designed for obstructive sleep apnea. They have no role in central sleep apnea.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Supplemental oxygen alone may partially reduce central apneas but does not address the underlying ventilatory instability.

stem: A 35-year-old woman with a BMI of 24 kg/m2 presents with daytime fatigue and unrefreshing sleep. She denies snoring. She takes oxycodone daily for chronic back pain. A sleep study reveals an AHI of 18, with apneas occurring without respiratory effort. Which of the following is the most likely mechanism?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Decreased pharyngeal muscle tone from opioids

choices.label: C
choices.text: Opioid suppression of central respiratory drive

explanation.summary: This patient has central sleep apnea caused by chronic opioid use. Opioids suppress brainstem respiratory centers, leading to apneas without respiratory effort.
explanation.whyCorrect: Opioids are a well-established cause of central sleep apnea. They suppress brainstem respiratory centers, reducing central drive to breathe during sleep. The apneas without respiratory effort confirm the central nature. Normal BMI, absent snoring, and lack of anatomical abnormalities argue against obstructive causes.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Decreased pharyngeal muscle tone would produce obstructive apneas with continued respiratory effort. This patient has apneas without effort.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Fat deposition causes obstructive sleep apnea. This patient has a normal BMI, and her apneas are central.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Retrognathia causes obstructive sleep apnea with respiratory effort. This patient's apneas are central.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tonsillar hypertrophy causes obstructive sleep apnea with snoring and respiratory effort, neither of which is present.

stem: A 42-year-old man with moderate obstructive sleep apnea (AHI 20) cannot tolerate CPAP despite multiple mask adjustments. He has a BMI of 28 kg/m2 and normal tonsils. Which of the following is the most appropriate alternative therapy?

explanation.summary: Mandibular advancement devices are the recommended alternative for patients with mild to moderate OSA who cannot tolerate CPAP. They advance the mandible forward, enlarging the pharyngeal airway.
explanation.whyCorrect: Oral appliances such as mandibular advancement devices are recommended for patients with mild to moderate OSA who fail CPAP. They work by advancing the mandible and tongue forward, increasing pharyngeal space.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Adenotonsillectomy is first-line for children with OSA from tonsillar hypertrophy. This adult has normal tonsils.
whyWrongByChoice.C: BiPAP provides bilevel pressures for central sleep apnea or patients requiring higher inspiratory pressures, not for CPAP mask intolerance in OSA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Hypoglossal nerve stimulation is emerging therapy for selected patients who fail CPAP. An oral appliance trial is typically first.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tracheostomy is a last-resort treatment. It is not appropriate for moderate OSA with available alternatives.

stem: A 47-year-old man with untreated obstructive sleep apnea presents to the emergency department after a motor vehicle accident. He reports falling asleep at the wheel. His wife has been urging him to seek treatment for loud snoring. Which of the following is this event an example of?

explanation.summary: Untreated OSA causes fragmented sleep from recurrent arousals, leading to excessive daytime sleepiness that significantly increases motor vehicle accident risk.
explanation.whyCorrect: Excessive daytime sleepiness is a major consequence of fragmented sleep in untreated OSA. Patients have significantly increased risk of drowsy driving and motor vehicle accidents.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While OSA is associated with arrhythmias, the patient reports falling asleep, consistent with excessive daytime sleepiness rather than syncope.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Hypoxia-induced seizures would present with convulsive activity, not gradual sleep onset.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Narcolepsy is a separate disorder with cataplexy and sleep paralysis. This patient's sleepiness is from untreated OSA.
whyWrongByChoice.D: TIAs cause focal neurological deficits, not gradual sleep onset while driving.

stem: A 56-year-old man with OSA treated with CPAP for 3 years presents for follow-up. His wife reports resolved snoring and no apneic episodes. However, he has persistent morning headaches. An arterial blood gas shows PaCO2 of 50 mm Hg while awake. His BMI is 38 kg/m2. Which of the following best explains his persistent symptoms?

explanation.summary: Morning headaches and daytime hypercapnia despite well-controlled OSA suggest coexisting obesity hypoventilation syndrome, characterized by obesity with chronic daytime hypercapnia not explained by another condition.
explanation.whyCorrect: OHS is defined by obesity (BMI >=30), chronic awake hypoventilation (PaCO2 >45 mm Hg), and sleep-disordered breathing. It frequently coexists with OSA. Despite adequate CPAP controlling obstructive events, the chronic hypercapnia and morning headaches indicate OHS requiring additional management.

whyWrongByChoice.A: CPAP malfunction would result in recurrent obstructive events and snoring. The wife confirms these are resolved.
whyWrongByChoice.B: While CSA can coexist with OSA, awake hypercapnia is more consistent with OHS than isolated CSA.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Inadequate CPAP pressure would fail to resolve snoring and apneas, which are reported as resolved.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Morning headaches with awake hypercapnia are due to CO2 retention causing cerebral vasodilation, not coincidental tension headache.

stem: A 40-year-old man is referred for a sleep study. He has a BMI of 32 kg/m2 and reports loud snoring. The study can be done in a sleep laboratory or at home. Which of the following measurements is available with in-laboratory polysomnography but typically NOT with home sleep testing?

explanation.summary: Home sleep apnea testing devices typically do not include EEG, meaning they cannot reliably detect arousals or determine sleep stages, unlike in-laboratory polysomnography.
explanation.whyCorrect: In-laboratory PSG includes EEG monitoring for sleep staging and arousal detection. Most home devices lack EEG, so they cannot identify sleep stages or arousals, potentially underestimating the AHI since total sleep time cannot be accurately determined.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Airflow measurement is a core component of both in-laboratory PSG and home devices.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Heart rate monitoring is included in both settings through pulse oximetry or ECG.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Oxygen saturation via pulse oximetry is standard in both in-laboratory and home testing.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Many home devices include respiratory effort bands, though quality may vary. EEG is the major absent modality.

stem: A 68-year-old man with atrial fibrillation and congestive heart failure presents with worsening daytime sleepiness. His wife describes progressively deeper breathing, then shallower, followed by a pause, in a repeating cycle throughout the night. Which pathophysiological mechanism best explains this pattern?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Pharyngeal muscle collapse during REM sleep

choices.label: B
choices.text: Delayed chemoreceptor feedback from prolonged circulation

choices.label: C
choices.text: Diaphragmatic fatigue from chronic heart failure

choices.label: E
choices.text: Vagal nerve stimulation from atrial distension

explanation.summary: In heart failure, prolonged circulatory time delays blood gas information from reaching central chemoreceptors, creating an overshoot-undershoot ventilatory pattern manifesting as Cheyne-Stokes respiration.
explanation.whyCorrect: Heart failure causes prolonged circulation time, so changes in blood gas tensions take longer to reach brainstem chemoreceptors. This delay creates feedback loop instability: by the time the brain detects rising PaCO2, hyperventilation overshoots, dropping PaCO2 below the apneic threshold and causing apnea. The cycle repeats.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Pharyngeal muscle collapse is the mechanism of OSA. This patient has a crescendo-decrescendo pattern consistent with central sleep apnea.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Diaphragmatic fatigue does not produce cyclical crescendo-decrescendo breathing. CSA with heart failure is due to ventilatory control instability.
whyWrongByChoice.D: The key mechanism involves central CO2 chemoreceptors with delayed feedback, not peripheral oxygen chemoreceptors.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While atrial distension occurs in heart failure, vagal stimulation does not cause periodic breathing. The mechanism is delayed circulatory feedback.

stem: A 50-year-old man with severe OSA (AHI 45) has been on CPAP for 6 months but removes it after 2 hours nightly. He drinks 3 beers every evening and takes lorazepam for anxiety before bed. Which recommendation is most likely to improve his sleep apnea symptoms?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Add supplemental oxygen to CPAP circuit

choices.label: D
choices.text: Discontinue alcohol and sedatives before sleep

explanation.summary: Alcohol and benzodiazepines decrease pharyngeal muscle tone and suppress arousal responses, worsening OSA. Discontinuing these substances before sleep is a critical lifestyle modification.
explanation.whyCorrect: Both alcohol and benzodiazepines worsen OSA by decreasing upper airway muscle tone, promoting pharyngeal collapse, and increasing the arousal threshold. Discontinuing these is a key intervention.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Supplemental oxygen does not address upper airway obstruction. Addressing modifiable factors is the priority.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Increasing pressure does not compensate for limited use duration. Addressing alcohol and sedatives that worsen OSA is the priority.
whyWrongByChoice.C: With severe OSA (AHI 45), CPAP remains preferred. Addressing modifiable worsening factors should come first.
whyWrongByChoice.E: While auto-titrating CPAP may improve comfort, addressing alcohol and benzodiazepines that actively worsen OSA should be the first step.

stem: A 55-year-old man with obstructive sleep apnea undergoes echocardiogram showing right ventricular dilation, tricuspid regurgitation, and estimated pulmonary artery systolic pressure of 55 mm Hg. Which of the following best describes the pathophysiological link between his OSA and these cardiac findings?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Chronic intermittent hypoxia causing pulmonary vasoconstriction

choices.label: B
choices.text: Left ventricular diastolic dysfunction from obesity

choices.label: C
choices.text: Nocturnal hypertension causing aortic root dilation

choices.label: D
choices.text: Recurrent Valsalva maneuvers increasing right atrial pressure

explanation.summary: Chronic intermittent hypoxia from recurrent obstructive apneas causes hypoxic pulmonary vasoconstriction, leading over time to pulmonary hypertension, right ventricular dilation, and cor pulmonale.
explanation.whyCorrect: Recurrent hypoxia triggers pulmonary arteriolar vasoconstriction. Chronic exposure leads to vascular remodeling and sustained pulmonary hypertension. Increased RV afterload results in dilation and tricuspid regurgitation (cor pulmonale).

whyWrongByChoice.B: While obesity commonly coexists with OSA, the direct link between OSA and right heart findings is through pulmonary vasoconstriction from intermittent hypoxia.
whyWrongByChoice.C: OSA causes nocturnal BP surges, but right heart findings are from pulmonary hypertension, not aortic root pathology.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Negative intrathoracic pressure swings occur during obstructive events but are not Valsalva maneuvers. The primary mechanism is pulmonary hypertension from chronic hypoxia.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The tricuspid regurgitation is functional from RV dilation, not structural valve damage from inflammation.

stem: A 38-year-old woman with a BMI of 26 kg/m2 presents with excessive daytime sleepiness. Physical examination reveals a small, posteriorly displaced mandible. Her husband reports loud snoring. Polysomnography reveals an AHI of 12. Which anatomical finding most directly contributes to her condition?

explanation.summary: Retrognathia (posteriorly displaced mandible) narrows the retroglossal airway space, predisposing to pharyngeal collapse during sleep. This is a recognized structural risk factor for OSA, especially in non-obese patients.
explanation.whyCorrect: Retrognathia causes posterior displacement of the mandible and tongue base, reducing retroglossal airway space and predisposing to upper airway collapse during sleep. In this non-obese patient, the small, posteriorly displaced mandible is the most likely anatomical contributor.

whyWrongByChoice.A: A deviated nasal septum can worsen snoring but is not the primary mechanism causing pharyngeal collapse in a patient with documented retrognathia.
whyWrongByChoice.B: An enlarged thyroid can cause external airway compression but is not described in this patient.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Macroglossia can contribute to obstruction, but the examination specifically identifies retrognathia as the structural abnormality.
whyWrongByChoice.E: Tonsillar hypertrophy is the most common cause in children. This patient has retrognathia identified on exam, with no enlarged tonsils mentioned.

stem: A 60-year-old man with hypothyroidism and a BMI of 36 kg/m2 is diagnosed with obstructive sleep apnea. He asks his physician why sleeping on his back makes symptoms worse. Which of the following best explains the positional worsening?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Increased chest wall compliance when supine

choices.label: C
choices.text: Reduced diaphragmatic excursion from abdominal contents

choices.label: D
choices.text: Gravity displaces tongue posteriorly when supine

explanation.summary: In the supine position, gravity pulls the tongue and soft palate posteriorly toward the pharyngeal wall, narrowing the upper airway and increasing the likelihood of obstruction.
explanation.whyCorrect: When sleeping supine, gravity causes the tongue, soft palate, and pharyngeal tissues to fall posteriorly, narrowing the already compromised upper airway. This is why positional therapy (avoiding supine sleep) can be beneficial.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Venous return actually increases in the supine position. This is not the mechanism for OSA worsening.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Chest wall compliance is not significantly altered by position. The worsening is due to gravitational effects on upper airway soft tissues.
whyWrongByChoice.C: While abdominal contents can reduce diaphragmatic excursion in obese patients, the specific positional worsening of OSA is from posterior tongue displacement.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The vagus nerve does not compress the airway. Positional worsening is mechanical from gravity on pharyngeal tissues.

stem: A 44-year-old woman with acromegaly presents for evaluation of excessive daytime sleepiness and witnessed apneas. Her BMI is 27 kg/m2. Polysomnography confirms OSA with an AHI of 28. Which feature of acromegaly most directly contributes to her obstructive sleep apnea?

choices.label: B
choices.text: Growth hormone excess causing insulin resistance

explanation.summary: Acromegaly causes macroglossia and pharyngeal tissue enlargement from excess growth hormone, narrowing the upper airway and predisposing to obstructive sleep apnea.
explanation.whyCorrect: Excess growth hormone causes soft tissue hypertrophy throughout the body, including macroglossia and pharyngeal tissue enlargement. These narrow the upper airway, making it more susceptible to collapse during sleep.

whyWrongByChoice.A: While acromegaly can cause cardiomyopathy, fluid redistribution is not the primary mechanism of OSA. The cause is soft tissue hypertrophy.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Insulin resistance contributes to metabolic complications but does not directly cause upper airway obstruction.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Peripheral neuropathy would contribute to central hypoventilation, not obstructive sleep apnea from anatomical narrowing.
whyWrongByChoice.E: The pituitary gland is in the sella turcica, far from the brainstem. A pituitary adenoma does not compress the brainstem.

stem: A 70-year-old man with severe heart failure and central sleep apnea continues to have an elevated AHI with predominantly central events despite optimized heart failure medications and a trial of CPAP. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?

choices.label: C
choices.text: Refer for phrenic nerve stimulator placement

choices.label: E
choices.text: Switch to bilevel positive airway pressure

explanation.summary: For CSA patients with heart failure who fail CPAP despite optimized cardiac therapy, bilevel positive airway pressure (BiPAP) provides higher inspiratory pressures to support ventilation during central apneas.
explanation.whyCorrect: When CSA persists despite optimized heart failure therapy and CPAP trial, BiPAP is an appropriate escalation. BiPAP delivers higher inspiratory and lower expiratory pressures, more effectively supporting ventilation when central respiratory drive is insufficient.

whyWrongByChoice.A: Supplemental oxygen alone does not adequately address central apneas or provide ventilatory support during absent respiratory drive.
whyWrongByChoice.B: Discontinuing positive airway pressure would worsen hypoxemia and sleep quality.
whyWrongByChoice.C: Phrenic nerve stimulation is emerging therapy but not yet standard of care. BiPAP should be trialed first.
whyWrongByChoice.D: Uvulopalatopharyngoplasty treats obstructive sleep apnea by removing pharyngeal tissue. It has no role in central sleep apnea.
